content.text: Errors are an inevitable part of any program — network requests fail, users input unexpected data, and code has bugs. JavaScript's

syntax provides a structured way to intercept those errors, respond to them, and clean up resources regardless of outcome. React builds on top of this with Error Boundaries and error states, but understanding synchronous error handling first is essential.

attrs.alt: Flowchart showing try/catch/finally execution: try block runs, error thrown branches to catch, finally always runs, error either continues or propagates up
attrs.title: try / catch / finally Execution Flow

content.text: — wraps the risky code. If any statement inside throws, execution jumps immediately to
— receives whatever was thrown. Runs only when an error occurred.

subclasses for different failure modes. Knowing which one you expect helps write targeted catch handlers.
You can throw any value with
— a string, a number, or an object — but convention is to always throw an
instance. Error objects carry a stack trace; plain values do not.
creates named error types that carry extra data and can be targeted with
block catches everything thrown by the
block. If you can only handle certain error types, re-throw the rest so they are not silently swallowed.
block is ideal for resource cleanup — closing connections, hiding spinners, releasing locks — because it runs regardless of success or failure.
— this is almost never what you intend. Keep
For operations that can fail (form validation, JSON parsing, local storage reads), catch the error inside the component and store it in state so you can display a meaningful message.
can throw in private browsing mode or if storage is full. Wrapping with
If you only want to run code when an error occurs but do not need the error value itself, ES2019 lets you omit the binding parameter.
A catch block that handles every possible exception can mask bugs. Catch only the errors you expect; re-throw the rest.
Throwing and catching exceptions purely for conditional branching is slow and unreadable. Use
for normal code paths and reserve
try/catch does not work with async code without await
blocks to handle different steps of an operation independently.
does not catch errors that occur
(inside JSX evaluation or lifecycle methods). For those, React uses
, allowing you to preserve the original error while wrapping it in a higher-level one. This keeps the full error chain visible in logs without losing context.
Cause chaining is especially valuable in React when an API layer wraps network errors — you surface a user-friendly message while still logging the root cause for debugging.
A common pattern in React apps is parsing untrusted data (JSON from
, query strings, API responses). A small utility centralises the error handling so callers stay clean:
variant lets you layer schema validation (e.g. Zod, Yup) on top — if the parsed value doesn't match the expected shape, it falls back gracefully instead of propagating.
Testing error paths is just as important as testing happy paths. Vitest provides helpers for asserting that functions throw:
directly lets the error escape before Vitest can catch it.
Production React apps typically send unhandled errors to a monitoring service. A thin wrapper keeps the rest of the codebase clean:
is a last-resort safety net — it catches any Promise rejection that wasn't caught by a
handler or try/catch. Always pair it with your error boundary for full coverage.
instances (not strings or plain objects) to get stack traces.

checks in catch handlers to respond differently to different failures.
Re-throw errors you cannot handle so they surface higher in the call stack rather than disappearing.
